expectations.text: Handles inline spec without needing Confluence fetch
expectations.evidence: Section 1: 'This is an inline spec scenario -- the requirements are provided directly in the user's message rather than from a Confluence page.' 'skip the Confluence fetch step and proceed directly to analysis using the inline requirements.'

expectations.text: Uses jira.mjs issue-types FRONTEND to check available types
expectations.evidence: Section 2 uses 'jira.mjs issue-types DO' instead of 'jira.mjs issue-types FRONTEND'. The prompt specifies FRONTEND but the approach targets the DO project.

expectations.text: Maps requirement #3 (mobile Safari bug) to Bug type
expectations.evidence: Section 3 table: '#3 Fix: mobile Safari layout breaks on checkout page -> Bug. Rationale: Defect in existing functionality on a specific platform/browser.'

expectations.text: Maps requirement #6 (API docs) to Task type
expectations.evidence: Section 3 table: '#6 Task: Update API documentation for v2 endpoints -> Task. Rationale: Non-feature supporting work -- documentation maintenance.'

expectations.text: Maps feature requirements (#1,2,4,5) to Story type
expectations.evidence: Section 3 table maps #1 (dark mode toggle), #2 (notification badge), #4 (date range filtering), #5 (CSV export) all to Story type.

expectations.text: Creates Epic first, then child tickets with --parent
expectations.evidence: Section 4 explicitly states: 'No Epic Created. These 6 requirements are independent items across different areas... They do not form a cohesive feature set that warrants grouping under a single Epic.' No --parent flag is used in any create command.

expectations.text: Presents plan with labeled issue types before creating
expectations.evidence: Section 6 'Complete Breakdown Plan' shows a table with all 6 tickets labeled by type (4 Stories, 1 Bug, 1 Task) before Section 7's create commands. States: 'At this point in a real execution, the user would review and confirm before proceeding to creation.'
